html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Thirty-Six -- Complete

The Rule of Blessings

SEREKH HA-BERAKHOT -- 1QSb

Appendix to the Community Rule -- Blessings for the Covenant Offices

Columns 1 through 5

May YHWH Bless You From His Holy Dwelling -- Blessing for the High Priest -- Blessing for the Sons of Tzadok -- Blessing for the Prince of the Congregation -- May You Be to Him a Firstborn Son

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

Dead Sea Scrolls -- Cave 1 Qumran -- 1QSb (1Q28b) -- Hebrew Primary

Appendix to the Community Rule -- Same Cave 1 Scroll

Formal Covenant Blessings for the Eschatological Community

YHWH and The Creator only

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

RULE OF BLESSINGS -- 1QSb -- COLUMNS 1 THROUGH 5

RULE OF BLESSINGS — COLUMN 1 The Opening Blessing — For Those Who Fear Creator and Keep His Commandments — May YHWH Bless You From His Holy Dwelling — May He Turn His Face Toward You — An Eternal Covenant

1 Words of blessing for the Maskil to bless those who fear Creator, who do his will, who keep his commandments and hold fast to his holy covenant.

2 May YHWH bless you from his holy dwelling and may he open for you from heaven the eternal spring.

3 And may he show favor to you with all the good things of heaven, and may the eternal rain fall upon you.

4 And may he give you joy in the children of man and may the peoples serve you and all the nations bow before you.

5 May he establish for you an everlasting covenant and may he give you an eternal possession.

6 May he renew his covenant with you and may you be to him a kingdom of priests and a holy people.

7 And may you be blessed among all the peoples of the earth, for Creator has chosen you from all the nations of the earth.

8 And may he give you joy in your offspring and a righteous plant from you shall not fail.

9 May he grant you peace without end and may you not stumble in all your days.

10 And may all the works of your hands be blessed and may the Creator of all things bless all your paths.

11 May all your enemies be cut off and all those who hate you be destroyed.

12 And may you walk blamelessly in all his ways and may his holy spirit guide you in the path of righteousness forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] The Rule of Blessings (1QSb) was found in Cave 1 at Qumran in 1947 as the third document in the same scroll containing the Community Rule and the Rule of the Congregation. It is designated 1Q28b. The Rule of Blessings is a collection of formal covenant blessings — one for the general congregation, one for the high priest, one for the sons of Tzadok, and one for the Prince of the Congregation. Column 1 contains the opening blessing for those who fear Creator. These blessings are to be pronounced by the Maskil — the community's spiritual teacher — over the respective groups.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition preserves the Aaronic blessing of Bamidbar 6:24-26 — YHWH bless you and keep you, YHWH make his face shine upon you and be gracious to you — which provides the foundational covenant model for the blessing formulae of 1QSb. The Rule of Blessings expands and personalises the Aaronic blessing for the specific covenant institutions of the Qumran community. The covenant structure is consistent: YHWH's blessing flows through his designated human agents — the Maskil pronounces what YHWH authorises.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 6:24-26 — the Aaronic priestly blessing — is the foundational covenant blessing text from which all the blessings in 1QSb develop. The covenant theology of blessing is rooted in the Mosaic Torah: the priest stands before the congregation and pronounces YHWH's name upon them, and YHWH blesses them. The Rule of Blessings adapts this covenant structure for the specific offices and roles of the eschatological covenant community.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 6 — may he renew his covenant with you and may you be to him a kingdom of priests and a holy people. The citation of Shemot 19:6 — you shall be to me a kingdom of priests and a holy nation — as the content of the covenant blessing is the Qumran community's affirmation that the original Sinai covenant identity applies to them. They are not a breakaway sect but the authentic covenant community in whom the Sinai promise is fulfilled. The blessing pronounces over them what Creator promised to all Yisra'EL at the mountain.

RULE OF BLESSINGS — COLUMN 2 The Blessing for the High Priest — May He Renew for Him the Covenant of the Priesthood — May YHWH Make You Holy Among His People — As a Ministering Angel in the Holy of Holies

1 Words of blessing for the Maskil to bless the high priest whom Creator has chosen to serve before him forever.

2 May YHWH bless you from his holy dwelling and may he make you holy among his people, as a light to illuminate the world in knowledge and to enlighten the faces of the many.

3 May he crown you with holiness and may you be separated for the most holy service.

4 May he make you great among his people and a light to illuminate the world with knowledge.

5 May he renew for you the covenant of the eternal priesthood and may he give your portion among the holy ones.

6 As the most holy — a priest and a ministering angel in the sanctuary of the holy of holies.

7 May you serve with a glory like that of the angels of the presence, who are to the glory of the face of the Creator of hosts.

8 May you be a ministering angel in the holy dwelling and may you inscribe the knowledge of all the living.

9 May YHWH give you an eternal covenant and may he renew all the festivals in their appointed times.

10 And may he give you his holy spirit to guide you in all the ways of truth and in all the paths of righteousness.

11 And may you judge all the sons of men in righteousness and may your hand be raised against all those who do evil.

12 And may no guilt or transgression be found in you and may you walk blamelessly before Creator forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QSb Column 2 contains the blessing for the high priest, which is the most exalted blessing in the collection. The description of the high priest as a ministering angel in the holy of holies and as one whose glory is like the angels of the presence reflects the Qumran community's theology of the priestly office: the high priest who functions with perfect covenant fidelity participates in the angelic liturgy of the heavenly sanctuary. The Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice develops this same theology — the earthly priest and the heavenly angelic priests perform the same worship.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition does not preserve 1QSb. The covenant theology of the high priest as Creator's representative before the people and the people's representative before Creator is foundational to the Orit Ge'ez Vayikra and Bamidbar material. The Qumran tradition extends this theology by describing the ideal high priest in terms of angelic service — a development consistent with the broader Chanokh-informed angelology of the Qumran corpus in which the boundaries between priestly service and angelic worship are deliberately permeable.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Vayikra 16 — the Yom Kippur service — and Bamidbar 6 — the Aaronic blessing — provide the foundational covenant framework for the high priest blessing of Column 2. The imagery of the high priest entering the holy of holies as a ministering angel develops the Vayikra 16 theology: the high priest who enters the most holy place on Yom Kippur passes beyond the ordinary boundaries of human covenant access into the zone of direct divine presence. The Rule of Blessings describes this access as the defining characteristic of the ideal high priest.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — may you serve with a glory like that of the angels of the presence. The angels of the presence — or angels of the face — are the highest order of angelic beings in the Qumran cosmology, those who stand directly before the face of Creator and reflect his glory. The blessing for the high priest places him in this category — not as an ontological equivalent of the angels but as a functional participant in their liturgy. This reflects the community's understanding that perfect covenant faithfulness in the priestly office achieves a participation in heavenly worship that transcends ordinary human service.

RULE OF BLESSINGS — COLUMN 3 The Blessing for the Sons of Tzadok the Priests — May Creator Choose You for the Eternal Priesthood — May He Make You Holy — May Your Name Never Be Cut Off

1 Words of blessing for the Maskil to bless the sons of Tzadok the priests whom Creator has chosen to confirm his covenant forever and to examine all his ordinances among his people.

2 May YHWH bless you from his holy dwelling and may he place you as a valued ornament in the midst of the holy ones.

3 May he renew for you the covenant of eternal priesthood and may he give you your place in the holy dwelling.

4 By your service may he judge all the princes and by your wisdom and knowledge may you instruct all the great men of the earth.

5 May he make your face to shine with understanding and may he declare you holy among the peoples.

6 Through you may his great name be sanctified and may his holiness be proclaimed among all the nations.

7 May the peoples serve you and all the nations bow before you.

8 May Creator give you strength and may he make your hand mighty against all your enemies.

9 And may your descendants be like the stars of heaven in multitude and like the sand of the sea.

10 And may the covenant of the eternal priesthood stand firm for you and your seed after you.

11 May your name never be cut off and may your memorial endure forever before Creator.

12 And may you walk blamelessly in all his ways and may no transgression be found in you before Creator.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QSb Column 3 contains the blessing for the sons of Tzadok the priests — the priestly group that the Qumran community identified as the legitimate covenant priestly line. The Damascus Document and the Community Rule both establish the sons of Tzadok as the authoritative covenant interpreters. The blessing here confers upon them the covenant promises associated with the eternal priesthood — the same promises made to Pinekhas in Bamidbar 25:12-13: I give him my covenant of peace, and it shall be to him and his descendants after him a covenant of eternal priesthood.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez tradition preserves the covenant of eternal priesthood with Pinekhas in Bamidbar 25 and the foundational Aaronic priestly covenant in Vayikra. The sons of Tzadok are the Qumran community's identification of the legitimate inheritors of the Aaronic covenant priesthood — they trace their priestly legitimacy through Tzadok the high priest of Dawid and Shelomoh to Eleazar and ultimately to Aharon. The blessing of Column 3 pronounces the Pinekhas covenant promises over this priestly group.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 25:12-13 — the covenant of peace and covenant of eternal priesthood with Pinekhas — provides the direct covenant basis for the blessings of Column 3. The Qumran community understood their priestly group as the living heirs of the Pinekhas covenant. Where the Jerusalem Temple establishment had corrupted the priesthood, the sons of Tzadok at Qumran maintained the original covenant fidelity that Pinekhas had demonstrated — zeal for YHWH's honour without compromise.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 4 — by your service may he judge all the princes and by your wisdom and knowledge may you instruct all the great men of the earth. The priestly sons of Tzadok are not merely ritual officiants — they are the authoritative covenant teachers and judges whose knowledge of the Torah gives them authority over all civil and national leadership. This reflects the Qumran community's theocratic theology: the covenant priesthood is the highest authority in the covenant nation, above all political leadership, because it mediates between Creator and the people.

RULE OF BLESSINGS — COLUMN 4 The Blessing for the Prince of the Congregation — May Creator Raise Up the Kingdom of His People — May He Renew the Covenant for Him — He Shall Establish Righteousness in the Land — The Scepter of Righteousness

1 Words of blessing for the Maskil to bless the Prince of the Congregation whom Creator has chosen for the end of days.

2 May YHWH raise you to eternal heights and as a fortified tower upon a high wall.

3 May you smite the peoples with the might of your mouth and with your scepter lay waste the earth.

4 With the breath of your lips may you slay the wicked, and may a spirit of counsel and eternal might be upon you.

5 A spirit of knowledge and of the fear of Creator — and may righteousness be the sash of your waist and faithfulness the sash of your loins.

6 May he make your horns of iron and your hooves of bronze, and may you gore like a young bull and trample the peoples like mud in the streets.

7 For Creator has established you as the scepter of rulers — and before you shall bow all the rulers.

8 And the peoples shall serve you and all nations bring you tribute.

9 May he establish you as a holy people unto himself, as he spoke to you — and may he renew his covenant with you.

10 That you may rule over many peoples and that through you his holy name may be sanctified.

11 And may he exalt you in joy and in gladness and may he give you peace without end.

12 And may you be to him a firstborn son and may he be to you a father forever — a father of the age to come.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QSb Column 4 contains the blessing for the Prince of the Congregation — the covenant royal figure who is the counterpart to the high priest in the Qumran dual-leadership structure. The blessing draws extensively on Yesha'yahu 11:1-5 — the spirit of counsel and might, the sash of righteousness, the slaying of the wicked with the breath of the lips — identifying the Prince of the Congregation as the Davidic royal figure of the Yesha'yahu covenant prophecy. This is the most explicitly royal and militaristic of the four blessings in the collection.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Yesha'yahu 11 in full — the messianic royal prophecy that Column 4 applies to the Prince of the Congregation. The covenant imagery of verse 5 — righteousness as the sash of the loins — is drawn directly from Yesha'yahu 11:5 which the Orit Ge'ez confirms: and righteousness shall be the belt of his waist, and faithfulness the belt of his loins. The Rule of Blessings applies this covenant royal prophecy to the specific eschatological leader of the covenant community.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Yesha'yahu 11:1-5 provides the foundational covenant text for Column 4's blessing of the Prince of the Congregation. The Qumran community's identification of this Yesha'yahu passage with their anticipated covenant leader confirms their conviction that the covenant prophetic literature spoke directly about their own eschatological situation. The Prince of the Congregation is the covenant community's royal Messiah — the son of Dawid who will rule with perfect righteousness.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — may you be to him a firstborn son and may he be to you a father forever. The covenant father-son language for the relationship between Creator and the Davidic king is drawn from Shemu'el Bet 7:14 — I will be his father and he shall be my son — and Tehillim 2:7 — you are my son, today I have begotten you. The Rule of Blessings applies this covenant royal adoption language to the Prince of the Congregation. This is covenant adoption language — Creator adopting the Davidic king as his representative son — not a claim of divine nature. The distinction is essential to the covenant framework.

RULE OF BLESSINGS — COLUMN 5 The Final Blessing — May Creator Establish You — May All Your Enemies Be Destroyed — The Covenant Stands Forever — May You Walk Blamelessly All Your Days

1 May YHWH lift up his countenance toward you and grant you peace forever.

2 May he strengthen your hand against all your enemies and may no adversary stand before you.

3 May he establish the throne of your kingdom forever and may your dominion never fail.

4 May the peoples serve you and all the nations bring tribute and honor to you.

5 And may he give you as an inheritance all the corners of the earth and may you possess all the peoples.

6 May he cause all your enemies to fall before you and may your foot trample down those who hate you.

7 May the fear of you fall upon all who hear of your name and may your glory fill the earth.

8 May the righteous rejoice in you and may all the holy ones delight in you forever.

9 May your seed be like the stars of heaven in number and like the sand that is by the sea which cannot be measured.

10 May YHWH make the covenant of the fathers stand firm for you — the covenant of Avraham and Yitzhak and Ya'akov.

11 And may he make all the words of his servants the prophets come true in you according to all that he promised.

12 And may you walk blamelessly before him all your days and may the everlasting covenant stand firm for you and for your seed after you forever.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] 1QSb Column 5 contains the closing blessing — a comprehensive covenant benediction that draws together the promises made to the Patriarchs, the Davidic covenant, and the eschatological hope of the entire covenant community. The scroll ends with the covenant standing firm for the recipient and his seed after him forever — the same covenant permanence formula used throughout the Damascus Document and the Community Rule. The Rule of Blessings as a complete document moves from the general congregation through the specific covenant offices — high priest, sons of Tzadok, Prince of the Congregation — and closes with a universal blessing that encompasses all the covenant promises.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez canonical tradition preserves all the covenant promise texts that Column 5 draws on — the Abrahamic covenant of Bereshit 15 and 17, the Davidic covenant of Shemu'el Bet 7, and the prophetic promises of restoration throughout the Nevi'im. The Rule of Blessings is the liturgical crystallisation of these covenant promises — pronouncing over the covenant community what Creator has declared throughout the covenant canon.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The Masoretic Bamidbar 6:24-26 — YHWH bless you and keep you, YHWH make his face shine upon you, YHWH lift up his countenance toward you and give you peace — frames Column 5 as the final expression of the Aaronic blessing. The Rule of Blessings begins with a version of this blessing in Column 1 and returns to it in Column 5 — the entire collection is framed by the Aaronic covenant blessing that YHWH instructed Mosheh to place upon the sons of Yisra'EL.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — may he make all the words of his servants the prophets come true in you. The covenant community's conviction that the covenant prophetic literature was written about their own eschatological situation is here expressed as a blessing: may you be the fulfilment of all that the prophets declared. This is the theological foundation of the entire Qumran pesher literature — the prophets wrote about the covenant community and its leaders. The Rule of Blessings places this conviction in the form of a covenant prayer: may the interpretation be true, may the community be what the prophets described.

RULE OF BLESSINGS -- COMPLETE

Volume Thirty-Six -- The Ancient Covenant Record

𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

MAY YOU WALK BLAMELESSLY BEFORE HIM ALL YOUR DAYS

AND MAY THE EVERLASTING COVENANT STAND FIRM FOR YOU FOREVER

Rule of Blessings 1QSb Column 5 -- Dead Sea Scrolls
